coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of multifunctional red-emissive carbon dots for fluorescent quercetin and pH sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jianhua
Lu, Wenjing
Meng, Yating
Wang, Huiping
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: A convenient and cost-effective hydrothermal route was developed for the synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) from neutral red and catechol. Superior properties of the R-CDs including high photostability, outstanding biocompatibility, and desirable biological penetration were found, which could realize the monitor of quercetin and pH. The quenching fluorescence of R-CDs was observed upon the addition of quercetin on account of a static quenching mechanism, achieving the concentration of quercetin in the scope of 0.66–111.33 μM with a detection limit of 0.19 μM. Furthermore, a smartphone based laboratory device and color processing software could be used for accurate testing of quercetin. Besides, the proposed method has been successfully quantified trace amounts of quercetin in red wine, lake water and human urine samples. Meanwhile, the R-CDs presented a pH response by both fluorescent and colorimetric mode. As the pH value increased, an excellent linear relationship was obtained against pH in the range of 6.20–7.60 accompanied with a significant color variation of the R-CDs solution from deep pink to yellow. Finally, the proposed R-CDs have also been applied to monitor the quercetin and pH in living cells due to its low cytotoxicity, providing a great capacity for various targets molecules detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: R-CDs
Quercetin
Static quenching mechanism
pH response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of pH by the fluorescence and colorimetric mode
Smartphone-assisted optosensing platform for monitoring of quercetin
Recovery test data of quercetin in red wine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and the inset HRTEM images of R-CDs. (b–d) The FT-IR spectra, XPS spectra, high-resolution O1s of R-CDs.

(a) The normalized UV–vis absorbance, fluorescence emission (red curve), and excitation (blue curve) spectra of the R-CDs (Insert: the pictures of R-CDs under 365 nm UV light). (b) Excitation − emission spectra of R-CDs (λEx/λEm = 444/605 nm).

(a–b) Selectivity and interference of R-CDs to quercetin with addition of various interferents: (1) L-Asn, (2) GSH, (3) L-Cys, (4) Met, (5) D-Arg, (6) L-Gly, (7) L-His, (8) L-Arg, (9) Glu, (10) AA, (11) DA, (12) Zn2+, (13) Ba2+, (14) Al3+, (15) Cu2+, (16) Fe3+, (17) Mg2+, (18) Ca2+, (19) Na+, (20) NO2 −, (21) SO3 2−, (22) Catechol, (23) Morin, (24) Resorcinol, (25) Rutin, (26) Quercetin.(100 μM for Quercetin, and 300 μM for other interferents). (c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with addition of quercetin. (0–146 μM). (d) Linear plot of (F0–F)/F0 versus quercetin concentrations. (e) Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs after the addition of quercetin. (f) UV–vis spectra of R-CDs aqueous solution, 33.3 μM quercetin + R-CDs compound aqueous solution(experimental and theoretical), and 33.3 μM quercetin aqueous solution.

(a) Fluorescence titration spectra of R-CDs over pH (2–11). (b) A fitted plot of FL intensity and pH (3.0–11.0) (c) Linear relationship between the fluorescence response and pH value (6.2–7.6). (d) Photographs of R-CDs with increased pH (3.0–9.0) under sunlight. (e) UV–vis spectra of R-CDs at different pH. (f) Abs ratio (A534nm/A453nm) versus the pH value.

(a) Images of R-CDs with increasing quercetin concentrations under UV light (365 nm). (b) Graphic of the RGB image analysis process and the formed color scale card. (c) Plot of fitting curve of fluorescence chroma value (RGB values) and concentrations of quercetin. Inset plot shows the RGB0/RGB values and the quercetin concentrations corresponding to (a).
(a) Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells stained by R-CDs and R-CDs incubated with 20 μL quercetin. (b) R-CDs (0.67 mg/mL) under different pH values (9.0, 7.4, and 4.0).
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of R-CDs and its simultaneous determination of quercetin and pH.
Analysis of quercetin in real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of multifunctional red-emissive carbon dots for fluorescent quercetin and pH sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient and cost-effective hydrothermal route was developed for the synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) from neutral red and catechol. Superior properties of the R-CDs including high photostability, outstanding biocompatibility, and desirable biological penetration were found, which could realize the monitor of quercetin and pH. The quenching fluorescence of R-CDs was observed upon the addition of quercetin on account of a static quenching mechanism, achieving the concentration of quercetin in the scope of 0.66–111.33 μM with a detection limit of 0.19 μM. Furthermore, a smartphone based laboratory device and color processing software could be used for accurate testing of quercetin. Besides, the proposed method has been successfully quantified trace amounts of quercetin in red wine, lake water and human urine samples. Meanwhile, the R-CDs presented a pH response by both fluorescent and colorimetric mode. As the pH value increased, an excellent linear relationship was obtained against pH in the range of 6.20–7.60 accompanied with a significant color variation of the R-CDs solution from deep pink to yellow. Finally, the proposed R-CDs have also been applied to monitor the quercetin and pH in living cells due to its low cytotoxicity, providing a great capacity for various targets molecules detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as promising fluorescent nanoparticles have garnered extensively interests owing to their superior fluorescence characteristics [1], good watersolubility [2], and biocompatibility [3], which make them exploited as fluorescent sensor for various target analytes determination, such as reactive oxygen species [4–6], metal ions [7,8], antibiotics [9,10], and so on. Until now, most of short-wavelength CDs with poor biological penetration (blue, green-emissive) were extensively prepared and applied. However, the serious shortcomings of short-wavelength CDs may severely restrict the practical capability of that CDs in optosensing and bioimaging [11]. To effectively enhance the detection efficiency, red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) with minor auto-fluorescence interference and excellent biological penetration could be introduced into above kind of system [12]. Therefore, it would be highly urgent to design and develop efficient and simple routes for the synthesis of R-CDs, expanding the application of R-CDs in biosensing fields.

Quercetin, plentiful in most plants and fruits, possesses extensive physiological effects including antioxidant, cardiovascular protection, and lipid peroxidation inhibitory effects [13]. As documented, quercetin as a traditional medicine could suppress the proliferation and growth of various cancer cells by regulating related cellular activities. However, it was reported that excessive intake of quercetin can pose negative impacts like kidney damage, reduction of enzyme activity, even DNA damage [14,15]. The conventional methods have been developed to monitor quercetin, such as mass spectrometry [16], HPLC [17,18], and ultraviolet (UV) spectrophotometry [19], which require complex pretreatment and professional operators. Considering this fact, the fluorescence analysis method represented by carbon dots for the determination of quercetin content appears to be necessary owing to its easy to use, good specificity, and high sensitivity. Zou et al. firstly synthesized organosilane-modified carbon dots (Si-CDs) for the analysis of quercetin on the basis of electrostatic interaction between Si-CDs and quercetin, which opened up a window for fluorescence sensing of quercetin [20]. The strong-emissive blue luminescent CDs were further exploited for analysis of quercetin in human serum and urine samples [21]. At present, some CDs have been explored to determine quercetin [22–24]. However, the signals of all these reported CDs based quercetin analysis methods in the blue or green channels may confine their application in bioimaging. Thus, it is desirable to develop CDs with long emission wavelengths for the assay of quercetin.

Intracellular pH plays an indispensable role in regulating the functions of various physiological activities, including cell proliferation [25], apoptosis [26], and endocytosis [27] etc. Variation of pH can lead to seriously numerous diseases, such as shock, rheumatoid arthritis, and even cancer [28–30]. For this reason, it is particularly necessary to precisely monitor intracellular pH fluctuation for the early diagnosis and prevention of diseases. Fluorescence assay based on CDs featuring noninvasiveness and real-time signals have been widely developed for intracellular pH sensing [31–34]. For example, Wang et al. [34] established a platform on the basis of multi-doped CDs for ratio fluorescence monitoring pH variations in the period of enzyme-catalyzed reactions. Liu et al. [31] developed a red emissive two-photon pH-CDs and demonstrated their capability for determination of pH in cells, tissue, and zebrafish. However, dual mode fluorescence/colorimetric pH-responsive CDs were rarely reported so far. Compared with the single mode analysis strategy, the dual-mode detection method can reduce the FL intensity variations by various external conditions [35]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop pH dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence sensors based on CDs.

Considering the demands mentioned above, red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) with both quercetin and pH sensitive behavior were prepared from neutral red and catechol through solvothermal method. The R-CDs presented high photostability, outstanding biocompatibility, and desirable biological penetration. Simultaneously, this red fluorescence quenching of R-CDs with addition of quercetin could be utilized for the determination of quercetin on account of static quenching. Meanwhile, the visual testing of quercetin could be realized by R-CDs based on color processing software on smartphone. Additionally, the FL intensity of the R-CDs exhibited a strong pH-dependence with a responsive linear range of 6.20–7.60. Meanwhile, pH variation could be easily visualized by an obvious color change of the R-CDs aqueous (from deep pink to yellow). The above system was further successfully exploited for assessing quercetin levels in real samples and monitoring the quercetin and pH fluctuations of living cells. (Scheme 1 ).

At first, neutral red (10.0 mg) and catechol (0.5 g) were mixed in 20 mL of double distilled water and ultrasound (10.0 min). Then, the mixture was heated in a Teflon autoclave at 180 °C for 3 h. After solvothermal reaction, the resulting solution was centrifuged (8000 rpm) for 10min and filtered (0.22 μm filter membrane). Subsequently, the supernatant was dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (100 Da) for 24 h. To end, rose-red powder was obtained by lyophilization for further testing.

To estimate the fluorescence response of the R-CDs towards quercetin, the R-CDs solution (100 μL, 2.0 mg/mL) were dispersed in phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). Various amounts of quercetin were introduced to R-CDs solution (0.67 mg/mL). And the final whole volume was kept at 3 mL. The solution was excited at 444 nm and the fluorescence emission spectra was collected in the wavelength range of 470–700 nm. To study the selectivity of R-CDs, common interferents (ions, amino acids and small molecules) were introduced into the R-CDs solutions under the same experimental conditions, respectively.

To test pH sensitivity, R-CDs solution was dispersed to 3.0 mL of phosphate buffer at a specific pH value and incubated for 3 min for the scan of fluorescence spectra (λex = 444 nm). The absorbance measurements were carried out with a UV spectrophotometer.

Red wine was obtained from a local supermarket (Wal-Mart supermarket, Taiyuan) and centrifuged for 10 min (10,000 rpm). After that, the supernatant was filtered (0.22 μm filter membrane) and diluted (100 times) before taking measurements, then stored at 4 °C applying for more test. Lake water was provided by Jinyang Lake (Taiyuan). Human urine samples were taken from volunteers in the Hospital of Shanxi University. Lake water and human urine samples were pretreated with micropore filter (0.22 μm) and diluted for 100 times. The pH value of all sample solutions was adjusted to 7.4. The feasibility of the analytical assay was investigated by the standard addition recovery method. A given concentrations of quercetin were introduced to above sample and measured via the aforementioned procedure.

For cell confocal imaging, HeLa cells were seeded on a confocal dish in DMEM (10% fetal bovine serum) at 37 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, R-CDs (0.67 mg/mL) were introduced to the DMEM encompassing living cells and cultured for 15 min. After that, the old medium was discarded after washing with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4). Instantly, the observation of HeLa cells was performed using a confocal microscope. To assess the selective intracellular imaging of quercetin, quercetin (20 μL) was introduced to the above system. Imaging of pH in cells: HeLa cells were treated with R-CDs in DMEM medium for 15 min, after which they were washed in PBS buffer and cultured with PBS buffer solutions of various pH values (9, 7.4, 4) in the presence of nigericin (5 μM) for 15 min. Thereafter, the cells were imaged using a confocal microscope.

The morphological structures of obtained R-CDs were characterized by TEM. Fig. 1 a displayed the as-synthesized R-CDs were well-dispersed nanospheres. Taking 50 particles into consideration, the particle size of R-CDs distributed from 0.5 nm to 5.0 nm and the average diameter was around 2.32 nm (Fig. S1). HRTEM images also showed a crystal spacing of 0.21 nm, matching with that of the (100) diffraction facets of graphite (Fig. 1a). The FTIR spectra interpreted chemical components of R-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1b, peaks near at 3454 cm− 1 and 3316 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of –OH/–NH2. It also presented the existence of CO (1628 cm−1) bonds in R-CDs. In addition, the peaks at 1522 cm−1 and 1357 cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibrations of C–N and phenolic hydroxyl group, respectively. The XPS spectrum also further confirmed the chemical bond of R-CDs. The XPS spectra of the R-CDs revealed major binding energy peaks: C1s (282.30 eV), N1s (399.70 eV), and O1s (529.90 eV) (Fig. 1c), which was consisted of C, N, and O elements with contents of 65.04%, 0.30%, and 29.15%, respectively. The C 1s peak-splitting results showed that there were four typical peaks at 284.50 eV, 285.16 eV, 286.10 eV, and 288.82 eV, belonging to CC, C–C, C–N, and CO (Fig. S2a). For N 1s, two peaks located at 399.10 eV and 400.15 eV, corresponded to C–NH2 and pyrrolic N, respectively (Fig. S2b). The O1s spectrum proved two fitting bands of CO (532.12 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (533.34 eV) (Fig. 1d). These results show that the surface-abundant hydrophilic groups can considerably enhance the water solubility of R-CDs, further providing active reaction sites.

As depicted in Fig. 2 a, two absorption peaks of R-CDs were found at 272 nm and 476 nm. The first one corresponded to the π-π* transition of sp2 hybridization (CC), while the wide absorption at about 476 nm was ascribed to the surface state of R-CDs. Under the optimal excitation of 444 nm, the R-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence at 605 nm. And the Stokes shift presented the large characteristic of 161 nm (Fig. 2a). Moreover, R-CDs possessed excitation-independent performance in the wavelength range of 474–700 nm, and the emission peak of the R-CDs were constant at about 605 nm under excitation from 444 nm to 564 nm (Fig. 2b). The optical stability of R-CDs was investigated at some conditions. The FL intensity of R-CDs did not show apparent fluctuation within ionic strength (less than or identical to 1.0 mol/L) and continuous irradiation with a Xe lamp (60 min) (Fig. S3a and S3b), indicative of excellent photostability of R-CDs. Moreover, the relative FLQY of R-CDs was determined to be 7.44% using choosing rhodamine 6G in ethanol. Above results clearly demonstrate that the R-CDs could be served as a prospective material for its practical application in complex systems.

First, the fluorescence sensing characteristics of R-CDs towards quercetin are studied. It is necessary to investigate the selectivity of R-CDs for their application in complex samples. As a result, the tested potential interferences had little influence and the R-CDs towards quercetin displayed the desired selectivity and ant interference ability (Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b). On increasing different amounts of quercetin, the fluorescence intensity at 605 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 3c). And the quenching efficiency displayed an excellent linearship (R2 = 0.9971) between R-CDs and quercetin in the concentration range of 0.66–111.33 μM (Fig. 3d). Also, the limit of detection was determined to be 0.19 μM (S/N = 3), being well comparable with other reported probes [18,21,23,36–38]. These results indicate that the RCDs have great specificity and high sensitivity towards quercetin and can be applied to quantify quercetin in practical sample analysis.

Furthermore, we explored the reaction mechanisms of R-CDs and quercetin. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of R-CDs did not overlap with the UV–vis absorption of quercetin (Fig.S4a), suggesting that the quercetin induced FL suppression could not be explained as the result of inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [39]. Next, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs without quercetin was measured as 1.15 ns, and it faintly changed to 1.10 ns with the addition of quercetin (Table S1), indicative of the presence of static quenching [40](Fig. 3e). Correspondingly, measurements of UV–vis spectra of the R-CDs showed that there was a noticeable peak shift after addition to quercetin (Fig. 3f). Simultaneously, the quenching constant Kq was measured as 7.19 × 1012 M−1 s−1, much larger than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1 (Fig. S4b) [41], revealing that a static quenching process has happened. Moreover, according to the “hard and soft acids and bases theory” (HSAB), the C–N and –NH2 on the surface of the R-CDs belong to soft base. Phenolic hydroxyl of quercetin belong to soft acid. A complex may have formed between R-CDs and quercetin by electrostatic interaction. And, the complex may constrain the transfer of non-radiative electron, along with reduction of fluorescence emission [23]. In a word, the sensing mechanism of quercetin based on R-CDs is attributed to the static quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of pH by the fluorescence and colorimetric mode
ce:section.ce:para: The R-CDs possess fascinating pH-dependent fluorescence and colorimetric performance. The optical property of R-CDs in PBS buffer solutions of different pH values was first investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the FL intensity at 605 nm enhanced with increasing pH (2.0–11.0). The pH response could be well fitted in a sigmoidal curve (Fig. 4b), an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.993) could be seen within the range 6.20–7.60 (Fig. 4c). The FL intensities of the R-CDs had no significant variations after adding to other ions under different pH (Fig. S5a), demonstrating the high selectivity of R-CDs to hydrogen ions. The fluorescence reversibility of R-CDs towards pH was further carried out. As depicted in Fig. S5b, the reversibility could retained after four cycles by adding acid or base between 7.4 and 4.0, suggesting that the fluorescence sensor was reversible. Time dependent FL intensity remained stable under different pH values (pH 4.0, and 7.4) for 90 min (Fig. S5c). Therefore, such fluorescence changes suggest that R-CDs can be a promising candidate for pH sensing in living cells.

Meanwhile, the gradual variation of the R-CDs solution at various pH value was consistent with the increasingly color transition from deep pink to yellow under sunlight (Fig. 4d). Fig. 4e showed the absorption peak intensity of 534 nm gradually decreased with increased pH (3.0–9.0), concurrently the absorption peak at 453 nm was observed to appear and gradually increased. The absorbance intensity ratio (A534/A453) varied linearly with pH value ranging from 5.2 to 7.8 (Fig. 4f). Above results indicate that R-CDs can be applied for identifying pH values in the colorimetric mode. As shown in Table S3, the fluorescence and colorimetric method has obvious advantages compared with the reported methods based on fluorescence probes for the detection of pH [42–46].

We next investigated the mechanism of R-CDs with pH-dependent property. The reason R-CDs exhibited pH-responsive property was that the variation with increasing pH value causing the variations in π−π* and n−π* transition of R-CDs [47,48]. The absorption in the long-wavelength area slowly decreased in the pH range 3–9 (Fig. 4e), leading to less self-absorption and increasing the red fluorescence of the R-CDs due to the shift of Fermi level [49]. To further investigate this influence, R-CDs solution at different pH values was then characterized by zeta potential analysis. The zeta potential of R-CDs gradually decreased from −3.04 mV to −17.40 mV with the pH increasing from 4.0 to 7.4 (Fig. S5d), which was consistent with our assumption. The dependence of fluorescence performance to pH may be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups (−OH/−NH2) on the surface of the R-CDs, which led to in the shift of the Fermi level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted optosensing platform for monitoring of quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: To realize the on-site quantitative analysis of quercetin content, a low-cost portable device combined with smartphone was constructed. The fluorescent images of R-CDs solutions with a series of concentrations of quercetin were clearly recorded using the smartphone camera under irradiation of UV light (365 nm). Then, the RGB values of the fluorescent images were transformed to matching color scale card and estimated with a smartphone app (color desk) (Fig. 5 b). As the concentration of quercetin was increased, the red color of the photographs became darker (Fig. 5a), indicating that increasing quercetin contents led to more fluorescence decrease. There was a favorable linear relationship (R2 = 0.9919) between sum RGB values and the quercetin concentrations (Fig. 5c). The LOD was calculated to be 1.63 μM (3σ/slope), which was sensitive sufficient to meet the requirements of real sample testing. The portable handheld device has comparable analytical behavior to the aforementioned fluorescence spectral analysis. Therefore, these results indicate that the smartphone based portable biosensor possesses a great potential for portable on-site quantitative monitoring of quercetin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery test data of quercetin in red wine samples
ce:section.ce:para: From a practical perspective, a certain amount of quercetin was introduced to red wine, lake water, urine and then evaluated by the established quercetin sensing method. The recovery rate was between 97.03% and 119.48% with the RSD of 3.35% for the recovery experiments (Table 1 ), manifesting the high reliability of the method. The results suggested the practicality and feasibility of the R-CDs for the quantitative analysis of quercetin in practical samples.

Prior of cells imaging, it is enormously essential to analyze the toxicity of the R-CDs using MTT assay. Even though the concentration of R-CDs was higher than 0.67 mg/mL, above 86.9% of the cell viability proved R-CDs with low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility (Fig. S6). Fig. 6 a proved that HeLa cells emitted red fluorescence after incubating with R-CDs. Followed by the addition of quercetin, the decreasing red fluorescence was observed (Fig. 6a). Then, we investigated the ability of R-CDs to evaluate the variation of intracellular pH. HeLa cells were treated with R-CDs for 15 min, and then PBS buffers at various pH (9.0, 7.4 and 4.0) were introduced and cultured for 15 min. As shown in Fig. 6b, there was a clear reduction in the red fluorescence as pH decreased from 9.0 to 4.0. At a lower pH value (pH = 4.0), the red fluorescence was extremely weak. The consequences obtained indicate that R-CDs have desirable biocompatibility and can monitor the fluctuations of quercetin and pH in living cells.

In conclusion, red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) was developed for simultaneously assay of quercetin and pH. The R-CDs were synthesized from neutral red and catechol by simple hydrothermal treatment. A signal-off fluorescence method based on R-CDs was constructed to determine quercetin on the basis of static quenching effect. Concurrently, the visual analysis of quercetin could be realized by R-CDs based color processing software on smartphone. It also has been effectively utilized for the quantitative detection of quercetin in real samples. Moreover, the R-CDs showed a pH-dependent property by both fluorescence and colorimetric mode. Finally, the fluorescence system was further successfully utilized for monitoring the quercetin and pH fluctuations in living cells. Thus, the proposed method provides a valuable reference for exploiting a multifunctional sensing platform for practical applications in the determination and monitoring of quercetin and pH in red wine, lake water and human urine samples and living systems.

Jianhua Guo: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Wenjing Lu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Yating Meng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Huiping Wang: Investigation, Validation. Chuan Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Shaomin Shuang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province under Grant (No. 201901D211155).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110766.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-carbonization synthesis of highly-bright red/near-infrared carbon dots by solvent-free method
J Mater Chem C

Mater Sci Eng C

Coord Chem Rev

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang B, Song H, Qu X, Chang J, Yang B, Lu S. Carbon dots as a new class of nanomedicines: Opportunities and challenges. Coordination Chemistry Reviews. 2021;442:214010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual near-infrared-emissive luminescent nanoprobes for ratiometric luminescent monitoring of ClO– in living organisms
ACS Appl Mater Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao C, Zhou X, Xue M, Han C, Feng W, Li F. Dual Near-Infrared-Emissive Luminescent Nanoprobes for Ratiometric Luminescent Monitoring of ClO- in Living Organisms. ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces. 2019;11(17):15298-15305.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-Targeting carbon quantum dots for peroxynitrite detection and imaging in live cells
Anal Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bai Y, Wang Y, Cao L, Jiang Y, Li Y, Zou H, et al. Self-Targeting Carbon Quantum Dots for Peroxynitrite Detection and Imaging in Live Cells. Analytical Chemistry. 2021;93(49):16466-16473.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red emission nitrogen and zinc co-doped carbon dots as fluorescent sensor for reversible detection of peroxynitrite in living cells
Sensor Actuator B Chem

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and green synthesis of bifunctional carbon dots for detection of Cu2+ and ClO– in aqueous solution
ACS Sustainable Chem Eng

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for doxycycline based on the sensitization effect of bovine serum albumin
J Hazard Mater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding L, Zhao Y, Li H, Zhang Q, Yang W, Fu B, et al. A highly selective ratiometric fluorescent probe for doxycycline based on the sensitization effect of bovine serum albumin. Journal of Hazardous Materials. 2021;416:125759.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fluorescent probe based on N, B, F co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive determination of sulfathiazole
